request.question: Among film directors, who was more well-known for horror films, Eric Red or Howard Hughes?

title: Summa Corporation
Bangladesh Film Directors Association
Altic/Howard Hughes (METRORail station)
Eric Red
The Tights
The Amazing Howard Hughes
List of Cambodian films of 1966

Hughes XF-11

sentences: Summa Corporation was the name adopted for the business interests of Howard Hughes after he sold the tool division of Hughes Tool Company in 1972.
The tool division would merge with Baker International in 1987 to form Baker Hughes, the world's third-largest oil-services company.
The name "Summa", Latin for "highest", was allegedly chosen by several of Hughes' employees without consulting him first.
Hughes was allegedly dissatisfied, and preferred the name "HRH Properties" - with the initials standing for both "Howard Robard Hughes" and "Hughes Resort Hotels".
His suggestions were never implemented, and the company remained Summa for 18 years following Hughes' death.
Bangladesh Film Directors Association is the pan-national trade body of film directors in Bangladesh.
The Bangladesh Film Directors Association’s General Secretary is Badiul Alam Khokon.
Mushfiqur Rahman Gulzar is the president of Bangladesh Film Directors Association.
Altic/Howard Hughes is a light rail station in Houston, Texas on the METRORail system.
It is served by the Green Line and is located on Harrisburg Boulevard at Altic Street in the East End.
The station is named for Altic Street as well as Howard Hughes, who built a Hughes Tool Company factory in the area.

Eric Red (born Eric Joseph Durdaller; February 16, 1961) is an American screenwriter and director, best known for writing the horror films "The Hitcher" and "Near Dark", as well as writing and directing "Cohen and Tate".
The Tights are an English punk rock band from Worcester.
The band's only two singles were released by the Cherry Red Records label: "Bad Hearts" / "It" / "Cracked" (1978) and "Howard Hughes" / "China's Eternal" (1978).
After John Peel played "Bad Hearts" on BBC Radio 1, this single made it to number six in the UK punk chart.
Follow-up "Howard Hughes" reached number four in the same chart.
After playing their farewell gig at the Marquee Club, London, The Tights split in 1979.

The Amazing Howard Hughes is a 1977 American made-for-television biographical film about American aviation pioneer and filmmaker Howard Hughes, based on the book "Howard: The Amazing Mr. Hughes" by Hughes' business partner Noah Dietrich.
The film starred Tommy Lee Jones, Ed Flanders, and Tovah Feldshuh and was made a year after Hughes's death, in 1976, while aboard a private flight to Mexico.
It was originally broadcast in two parts on CBS on April 13 and 14, 1977.

Ros Serey Sothear becomes a popular artist for movie soundtracks among film directors, taking Huoy Mea's old position Of the 18 films listed, 4 films are in existence, 6 have been remade, and 8 have not yet been remade.
The Howard Hughes Corporation () is a major real estate development and management company based in Dallas, Texas.
It began as part of the oil drilling tool business founded by Howard R. Hughes, Sr., in 1913, which under his son Howard Hughes, Jr., diversified into real estate development.
The real estate operations became a separate company, which was acquired by The Rouse Company, which in turn was acquired by General Growth Properties (GGP).
In 2010, GGP formed The Howard Hughes Corporation as a separate company.
The Hughes XF-11 was a prototype military reconnaissance aircraft, designed and flown by Howard Hughes and built by Hughes Aircraft for the United States Army Air Forces.
Although 100 F-11s were ordered in 1943, only two prototypes and a mockup were completed.
During the first XF-11 flight in 1946, Howard Hughes crashed the aircraft in Beverly Hills, California.
The production aircraft had been cancelled in May 1945, but the second prototype was completed and successfully flown in 1947.
The program was extremely controversial from the beginning, leading the U.S. Senate to investigate the F-11 and the Hughes H-4 Hercules flying boat in 1947–1948.

Howard Robard Hughes Jr. (December 24, 1905 – April 5, 1976) was an American businessman, investor, pilot, film director, and philanthropist, known during his lifetime as one of the most financially successful individuals in the world.
He first made a name for himself as a film producer, and then became an influential figure in the aviation industry.

Later in life, he became known for his eccentric behavior and reclusive lifestyle—oddities that were caused in part by a worsening obsessive–compulsive disorder (OCD) and chronic pain from a plane crash.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: Among film directors, who was more well-known for horror films, Eric Red or Howard Hughes?
hop1_docs: Eric Red | Eric Red (born Eric Joseph Durdaller; February 16, 1961) is an American screenwriter and director, best known for writing the horror films "The Hitcher" and "Near Dark", as well as writing and directing "Cohen and Tate".

Eric Brian Hughes | Eric Brian Hughes also known as E.B. Hughes, is an American writer, director and producer.  He has written, produced and directed two feature films, "Pacing the Cage" (2014) and "Turnabout" (2016).  His screenplay "The Fallen Faithful" won Best Screenplay at the 2011 Beverly Hills Film Festival.  Hughes is a member of the Producers Guild of America.

Howard Deutch | Howard Deutch (born September 14, 1950) is an American film and television director.  He is perhaps best known for his collaboration with filmmaker John Hughes, having directed two of Hughes' well-received screenplays, "Pretty in Pink" and "Some Kind of Wonderful".  Since 2010, he has primarily directed television productions, including multiple episodes of "Getting On" and "True Blood".

The Creature Walks Among Us | The Creature Walks Among Us, released in 1956, is the third and final installment of the "Creature from the Black Lagoon" horror film series from Universal Pictures, following 1955's "Revenge of the Creature".  The film was directed by John Sherwood, the long-time Universal-International assistant director, in his directorial debut.  Jack Arnold, who had directed the first two films in the series, had moved on to "A-list" films, and felt he had no more to contribute to the horror genre.  He suggested that his assistant director, Sherwood, could move up to full director, which partly affected Universal's decision to allow him to direct the film.  "The Creature Walks Among Us" starred Jeff Morrow, Rex Reason, and Leigh Snowden, and, like the original "Creature from the Black Lagoon", had music composed by Henry Mancini, who at the time was under contract with Universal.

Vernon Zimmerman | Vernon Zimmerman is an American writer and director who made his feature debut as director with the 1962 short "Lemon Hearts" starring Andy Warhol associate Taylor Mead.  Zimmerman wrote and directed the Claudia Jennings roller derby drive-in film "Unholy Rollers" and collaborated with Terrence Malick on the script to the road movie "Deadhead Miles".  He is most well known for his horror slasher film "Fade to Black", a dark and despairing psychological study of an awkward and alienated hardcore film buff who exacts a harsh revenge on his cruel tormentors.  Zimmerman received a Saturn Award nomination as Best Director for the film, a predecessor to more well-known modern parodies of the horror genre.  Vernon also wrote the scripts for the horror-Western "Hex" (a.k.a. "Charmed"), the redneck crime exploitation film "Bobbie Jo and the Outlaw", the made-for-TV wrestling comedy/drama "Mad Bull", the failed TV pilot film "Shooting Stars", and the teen fantasy comedy "Teen Witch".  Zimmerman's latest film is the six-minute comic short "Chuck and Wally on the Road".  More recently Vernon has been working as a script analyst.  He also teaches screen-writing courses at UCLA's Extension and Certificate Program.  Zimmerman also taught classes on both writing feature scripts and directing actors for film and television at the USC School of Cinema and Television.  Vernon Zimmerman lives in Los Angeles and is a member of both the Writers Guild of America and the Directors Guild of America.

Inside (2007 film) | Inside (French: "À l'intérieur" ) is a 2007 French home invasion horror film directed by Julien Maury and Alexandre Bustillo, starring Alysson Paradis and Béatrice Dalle.  It was written by co-director Bustillo, and is the first feature film from either director.  It concerns the attack and home invasion of a young pregnant woman by a mysterious stranger who seeks to take her unborn baby.  The film received generally positive reviews from mainstream critics upon its release and was particularly well received among horror film critics, noting it for being a genuinely scary and brutally violent example of the new wave of French horror.

Voice (film) | Voice ( also known as Whispering Corridors 4: Voice and Voice Letter) is a 2005 South Korean horror film, and the fourth installment of the "Whispering Corridors" film series.  This film was the debut film for its three young actresses, as well as director Choi Ik-Hwan, who had served as an assistant director on the first film of the series.
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Eric Red — passage [1]: "best known for writing the horror films 'The Hitcher' and 'Near Dark'" (explicit horror association).
  - Howard Hughes — not mentioned in the provided passages.
  - Howard Deutch (similar name) — passage [3]: "perhaps best known for ... 'Pretty in Pink' and 'Some Kind of Wonderful'" (no horror association).

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - Missing the intended "Howard Hughes" page or facts; possible name confusion with "Howard Deutch" (Deutch is present but is a different director).
  - No information in the passages about whether Howard Hughes worked on or was known for horror films.

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - Confirm which "Howard" is intended (Howard Hughes vs. Howard Deutch).
  - If Howard Hughes is intended: need his filmography/biography and any statements linking him to horror films (credits, "best known for" or genre association).
  - If Howard Deutch is intended: need an explicit statement about whether he is known for horror (passages already suggest he is not).

- retrieval_direction (priority):
  1. Howard Hughes — "Howard Hughes" (Wikipedia / biography / filmography) — check directing/producing credits and genre associations.
  2. If name confusion suspected: open "Howard Deutch" page (confirm genres he's known for).
  3. Search query: "Howard Hughes horror films" or "Howard Hughes director horror" to find any horror-related credits.
hop2_docs: The Amazing Howard Hughes | The Amazing Howard Hughes is a 1977 American made-for-television biographical film about American aviation pioneer and filmmaker Howard Hughes, based on the book "Howard: The Amazing Mr. Hughes" by Hughes' business partner Noah Dietrich.  The film starred Tommy Lee Jones, Ed Flanders, and Tovah Feldshuh and was made a year after Hughes's death, in 1976, while aboard a private flight to Mexico.  It was originally broadcast in two parts on CBS on April 13 and 14, 1977.

Flick (2008 film) | Flick is a campy British horror film written and directed by David Howard, and starring Hugh O'Conor and Faye Dunaway.  It had its theatrical release in 2008, and the DVD of the film was released in the United Kingdom on 19 October 2009.  The film was shot in and around Cardiff, Pontypool, Newbridge, Caerphilly, Briton Ferry Wales.

Cult of the Cobra | Cult of the Cobra is an independently made 1955 American black-and-white horror film, produced by Howard Pine, directed by Francis D. Lyon, that stars Faith Domergue, Richard Long, Kathleen Hughes, Marshall Thompson, Jack Kelly, William Reynolds, and David Janssen.  The film was released by Universal Pictures on a double bill with "Revenge of the Creature".

The Outlaw | The Outlaw is a 1943 American Western film, directed by Howard Hughes and starring Jack Buetel, Jane Russell, Thomas Mitchell, and Walter Huston.  Hughes also produced the film, while Howard Hawks served as an uncredited co-director.  The film is notable as Russell's breakthrough role, turning the young actress into a sex symbol and a Hollywood icon.  Later advertising billed Russell as the sole star.

The Awful Dr. Orloff | The Awful Dr. Orloff (Spanish: Gritos en la noche ) is a 1962 Spanish–French horror film directed by Jesús Franco.  It stars Howard Vernon as the mad Dr. Orloff (or sometimes Orlof) who wants to repair his disfigured daughter's face with skin grafts from others, with the aid of a slavish blind henchman named Morpho.  The film is considered to be the earliest Spanish horror film.  Howard Vernon continued to appear in a number of Franco's horror films up until his death.  Franco would later feature a number of blind or disfigured henchmen named Morpho in many of his later horror films, such as "Vampyos Lesbos" and "Revenge in the House of Usher".

Hell's Angels (film) | Hell's Angels is a 1930 independently made American epic aviation war film, directed and produced by Howard Hughes, that stars Ben Lyon, James Hall, and Jean Harlow.  The film, which was written by Harry Behn and Howard Estabrook, was released by United Artists.  Originally shot as a silent film, Hughes retooled "Hell's Angels" over a lengthy gestation period.  Most of the film is in black-and-white, but there is one color sequence, the only color footage of Harlow's career.

Rupert Hughes | Rupert Hughes (January 31, 1872 – September 9, 1956) was an American novelist, film director, Oscar-nominated screenwriter, military officer, and music composer.  He was the brother of Howard R. Hughes, Sr. and uncle of billionaire Howard R. Hughes, Jr. His three-volume scholarly biography of George Washington broke new ground in demythologizing Washington and was well received by historians.  A staunch anti-Communist, in the 1940s he served as president of the American Writers Association, a group of anti-Communist writers.
response.summary_2: Eric Red. Eric Red is best known for writing the horror films The Hitcher and Near Dark. Howard Hughes is associated with non-horror films such as the Western The Outlaw and the aviation/war film Hell's Angels.
